The **Arima Kinen** (有馬記念) is a [Grade I](/wiki/Conditions_races "Conditions races") [flat](/wiki/Flat_racing "Flat racing") [horse race](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing") in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") open to [Thoroughbreds](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred") three-years-old and above and the world's largest betting horserace.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is run on the fourth Sunday of December each year, over a distance of 2,500 metres (approximately 1 mile and 4+1⁄2 furlongs) at [Nakayama Racecourse](/wiki/Nakayama_Racecourse "Nakayama Racecourse"). It is one of the two "All-Star" races in Japanese horse racing; the other is the [Takarazuka Kinen](/wiki/Takarazuka_Kinen "Takarazuka Kinen") in late June.

The event was first run in 1956, and was initially titled the ***Nakayama Grand Prix*** (中山グランプリ). The following year it was renamed in memory of [Yoriyasu Arima](/wiki/Yoriyasu_Arima "Yoriyasu Arima") (1884–1957), the founder of the race. The distance was originally set at 2,600 metres, and shortened to the present length, 2,500 metres, in 1966.

The majority of the runners (10 out of 16) in the field are selected by a vote from racing fans, which must be a [Japan Racing Association](/wiki/Japan_Racing_Association "Japan Racing Association") horse. If any horse among the top 10 decided not to participate in the race, the void will be filled with next available horse until 10 available runners are filled. The remainder of 6 (including [National Association of Racing](/wiki/National_Association_of_Racing "National Association of Racing") (NAR) and foreign-based horses) are determined by the amount of prize money won.

Until 1999 the Arima Kinen was open to Japanese trained horses only. However, the [Japan Racing Association](/wiki/Japan_Racing_Association "Japan Racing Association") introduced a new condition in 2000 which allowed for the participation of a foreign trained horse, if it had won that year's [Japan Cup](/wiki/Japan_Cup "Japan Cup") (although no such eligible horse has participated in this race). The Arima Kinen was classed as a Domestic Grade I until 2006. It was then promoted to an International Grade I in 2007. Consequently, it is now possible for more foreign trained horses to compete in the race. The maximum number of these was set at four in 2007, and increased to six for the 2008 running.

## Records

* 2 – Speed Symboli (1969, 1970)

* 2 – [Oguri Cap](/wiki/Oguri_Cap "Oguri Cap") (1988, 1990)
* 2 – [Grass Wonder](/wiki/Grass_Wonder "Grass Wonder") (1998, 1999)
* 2 – [Symboli Kris S](/wiki/Symboli_Kris_S "Symboli Kris S") (2002, 2003)
* 2 – [Orfevre](/wiki/Orfevre "Orfevre") (2011, 2013)

**Most wins by a [jockey](/wiki/Jockey "Jockey"):**

* 4 – [Kenichi Ikezoe](/wiki/Kenichi_Ikezoe "Kenichi Ikezoe") (2009/Dream Journey, 2011, 2013/Orfevre, 2018/Blast Onepiece)
* 4 – [Yutaka Take](/wiki/Yutaka_Take "Yutaka Take") (1990/Oguri Cap, 2006/Deep Impact, 2017/Kitasan Black, 2023/Do Deuce)

**Most wins by a [trainer](/wiki/Horse_trainer "Horse trainer"):**

* 4 – Yasutoshi Ikee (2009, 2011, 2013, 2016)

**Most wins by an owner:**

* 7 – Sunday Racing (2009, 2011, 2013, 2014, 2020, 2024, 2025)
* **The youngest winning jockey:** Yutaka Take (1990 Oguri Cap, 21 Years old, 9 Months 9 Days)
* **The oldest winning jockey:** Yutaka Take (2023 Do Deuce, 54 Years old 9 Months 10 Days)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arima_Kinen&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The [TBS Television](/wiki/TBS_Television_(Japan) "TBS Television (Japan)") TV Series [*Passing the Reins*](/w/index.php?title=Passing_the_Reins_(TV_Series)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Passing the Reins (TV Series) (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%B6%E3%83%BB%E3%83%AD%E3%82%A4%E3%83%A4%E3%83%AB%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%83%9F%E3%83%AA%E3%83%BC "ja:ザ・ロイヤルファミリー")] revolves around the main characters aiming to win the Arima Kinen with a horse from [Hidaka](/wiki/Hidaka_Subprefecture "Hidaka Subprefecture").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In addition, a number of the races have been portrayed in *[Umamusume: Pretty Derby](/wiki/Umamusume:_Pretty_Derby "Umamusume: Pretty Derby")* in anime and manga by the personifications of the horses who participated in them:

* The 1988 race, which was [Tamamo Cross](/wiki/Tamamo_Cross "Tamamo Cross")' retirement race and [Oguri Cap](/wiki/Oguri_Cap "Oguri Cap")'s first and only win against Tamamo Cross, is portrayed in *Cinderella Gray*, which portrays Oguri Cap's racing career, in both manga and "Gray Phantom," the 22nd episode of Season 1 of its anime.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) The Nakayama Racecourse Horse Owners' Association also invited *Umamusume'*s voice actresses for Oguri Cap ([Tomoyo Takayanagi](/wiki/Tomoyo_Takayanagi "Tomoyo Takayanagi")), Tamamo Cross ([Naomi Ōzora](/wiki/Naomi_%C5%8Czora "Naomi Ōzora")) and [Super Creek](/wiki/Super_Creek "Super Creek") ([Kana Yūki](/wiki/Kana_Y%C5%ABki "Kana Yūki")) to attend the 2025 race coinciding with the airing of "Gray Phantom", and the three also participated in the award ceremony.[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* The 1990 race – Oguri Cap's retirement race – is also the final race of the *Cinderella Gray* manga.
* The 1993 race, which was [Tokai Teio](/wiki/Tokai_Teio "Tokai Teio")'s comeback and ultimately final race after a year injured, is portrayed in "Galloping After Our Dreams," the finale of the [second season](/wiki/List_of_Umamusume:_Pretty_Derby_episodes#Season_2_(2021) "List of Umamusume: Pretty Derby episodes") of the main *Umamusume: Pretty Derby* anime. The scene also incorporates the actual words of the broadcast call.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* The 2015 race, which was [Gold Ship](/wiki/Gold_Ship "Gold Ship")'s retirement race, is portrayed in "The Dream Never Ends", the 3rd episode of Season 3 of the main anime.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
* The 2016 race, which featured [Satono Diamond](/wiki/Satono_Diamond "Satono Diamond") winning the race and [Kitasan Black](/wiki/Kitasan_Black "Kitasan Black") coming in second, is portrayed in "Our Arima Kinen", the 7th episode of the same season.[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* The 2017 race, which was Kitasan Black's retirement race, is portrayed in "And Yours...", the 13th episode of the same season.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
